findings.merge_implication: For 4 of the 5 skills the runtime contract is already uniform and loop-type-parameterized — this is the strongest evidence the merge is low-risk for those four. A single merged skill maps cleanly onto the existing `--loop-type {context,research,review,council}` axis without touching the runtime. The merge should treat loop-type as the canonical mode discriminator already proven by these four consumers.

findings.area: deep-improvement is the OUTLIER — duplicates loop-host + reducer + convergence locally
findings.observation: deep-improvement does NOT consume the runtime CLI scripts at all. A grep of the whole skill for `fanout-`, `reduce-state` (runtime), `convergence`, `upsert.cjs`, `coverage-graph` returned NO runtime script invocations. Instead it ships its OWN loop driver `scripts/shared/loop-host.cjs` (a spawnSync-based dispatcher, loop-host.cjs:37,263) and its OWN reducer `scripts/shared/reduce-state.cjs`. Its command YAML wires `reducer: .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/scripts/shared/reduce-state.cjs` and `loop-host.cjs --mode=<lane>` — never a runtime script (deep_start-agent-improvement-loop_confirm.yaml:100,228). Its convergence/stop logic is local and differently-shaped: `reduce-state.cjs` contains NO `convergence|blockedStop|STOP_ALLOWED|saturation|agreementRate` tokens (zero matches), and SKILL.md describes a bespoke `blockedStop` legal-stop-gate model (SKILL.md:342) rather than the runtime's `convergence.cjs` composite-score path.
findings.merge_implication: This is the central merge risk and the prime fold-in candidate. deep-improvement reimplements loop orchestration (loop-host), state reduction (reduce-state), and convergence/stop that the runtime already provides for the other four. Folding it into deep-loop-workflows on the same runtime backend is NOT a pure docs/persona merge — it requires either (a) porting agent/model/skill/non-dev benchmark lanes onto runtime fanout+convergence+upsert (loop-type would need new kinds), or (b) explicitly carrying deep-improvement's local loop-host/reducer as a divergent execution path inside the merged skill. The phased spec must isolate deep-improvement as its own migration phase.

findings.area: Partial runtime coupling deep-improvement DOES have (state-safety library, not CLI)
findings.observation: deep-improvement is not fully isolated: its `reduce-state.cjs` deliberately prefers the runtime's state-safety LIBRARY helpers, in-process requiring `deep-loop-runtime/lib/deep-loop/atomic-state.ts` and `jsonl-repair.ts` via a tsx CJS register shim, computing `RUNTIME_DEEP_LOOP = path.join(__dirname,'..','..','..','deep-loop-runtime','lib','deep-loop')`. The comment names them 'the single source of truth for the atomic-write + jsonl-repair contracts.' Its `scripts/lib/typed-errors.cjs` also explicitly mirrors 'deep-loop-runtime's CLI guard pattern' (copied, not imported).
findings.merge_implication: deep-improvement already trusts the runtime as the state-safety source of truth at the LIBRARY level (atomic-state, jsonl-repair) even while owning its CLI orchestration. The merge can lean on this: the lib-level seam is clean and already shared, so the fold-in work is concentrated on the CLI/orchestration layer (loop-host, reducer, convergence), not on state-write safety. Note the typed-errors mirror is a COPY — a candidate to dedupe into the runtime during the merge.

findings.area: Library-level runtime imports beyond the CLI scripts
findings.observation: Beyond CLI scripts, several skills reference runtime LIBRARY modules that the merged skill must keep wired: `lib/deep-loop/executor-config.ts` (`parseExecutorConfig`, resolves native vs cli-codex vs cli-* dispatch branch — used by research and review loop_protocol), `lib/deep-loop/post-dispatch-validate.ts` (v2 strict seat-finding validation, review), `lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-signals.ts` (graph convergence handler, review `dimensionCoverage` threshold), `lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts` (`VALID_KINDS`, schema, weights — context `CONTEXT_WEIGHTS.REUSES=1.5`), and `lib/council/council-graph-db.ts` (council storage with unique constraints). The deep-loop durability layer (`atomic-state, jsonl-repair, loop-lock, post-dispatch-validate, executor-audit`) is documented as inherited by every loop.
findings.merge_implication: The runtime exposes a richer surface than the four CLI scripts: an executor-config resolver, a post-dispatch validator, per-loop-type coverage-graph signal handlers/weights, and a council-specific graph DB. The merged deep-loop-workflows skill must preserve per-mode references to these lib modules (they are loop-type-specialized: review has dimensionCoverage, context has REUSES weight, council has its own graph-db). A naive doc merge that collapses per-mode convergence/weight references would lose mode-specific behavior — the merge must keep mode-keyed pointers into the runtime lib.

findings.area: Storage / schema ownership lives in runtime
findings.observation: The coverage-graph SQLite DB (`deep-loop-graph.sqlite`) and its schema owner moved to the runtime (`.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/storage/` and `lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts`). The research changelog records that consumers previously importing from `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/{deep-loop,coverage-graph}/*` were repointed to `deep-loop-runtime/lib/*`, and the four `mcp__mk_spec_memory__deep_loop_graph_*` MCP tools were removed in favor of the runtime script paths (no aliases).
findings.merge_implication: Storage and schema are already cleanly runtime-owned and shared across loop types via a single DB keyed by (spec_folder, loop_type, session_id, iteration). The merge does not need to touch storage — the merged workflows skill writes through `upsert.cjs --loop-type <mode>`. This confirms the intended two-skill split (runtime=backend+storage, workflows=modes) already holds at the persistence layer for 4 of 5 modes; only deep-improvement uses a separate state store (its own reduce-state output), reinforcing it as the migration outlier.

open_questions: Does deep-improvement's own convergence/stop math (the `blockedStop` legal-stop-gate model in reduce-state's pipeline, distinct from runtime convergence.cjs) live in reduce-state.cjs or in a separate gate module (promotion-gates.cjs / rubric-guard.cjs were observed in scripts/lib and scripts/shared)? The exact file owning convergence for deep-improvement was not pinpointed in this slice.

Are the per-mode `fanout-merge.cjs` behaviors (research dedup vs review strongest-restriction vs context agreement vs council) selected by a `--loop-type` switch INSIDE one fanout-merge.cjs, or are there separate code paths? This determines whether the merged skill can rely on a single parameterized merge entry point.

Do deep-context/deep-research/deep-review SKILL.md bodies (not just command YAML) ever invoke runtime scripts directly, or is 100% of their runtime CLI consumption delegated to the command layer? Council was confirmed to shell out directly from a skill script; the other three appeared command-only in this slice but their SKILL.md scripts/ dirs were not exhaustively grepped for shell-outs.

Does deep-improvement's loop-host.cjs spawnSync dispatch duplicate the runtime fanout-run.cjs pool/concurrency logic, or is it single-lineage sequential? It showed a single spawnSync call site (loop-host.cjs:263) without an obvious pool — confirming whether parallel fan-out is absent would clarify the fold-in cost.

readers.slice: governance artifacts (feature_catalog/ + manual_testing_playbook/) across deep-ai-council, deep-context, deep-improvement, deep-research, deep-review

findings.observation: All 5 skills carry BOTH a feature_catalog/ and a manual_testing_playbook/ directory, each with a master index file plus NN--category/ subfolders of per-feature .md files. Structure is parallel but NOT identical: counts, category schemas, and feature-ID prefixes all differ per skill. Master files: deep-ai-council/feature_catalog/FEATURE_CATALOG.md (UPPERCASE), the other 4 use feature_catalog.md (lowercase); all 5 playbook masters are manual_testing_playbook/manual_testing_playbook.md (lowercase).
findings.merge_implication: A merged deep-loop-workflows skill needs ONE governance tree. The casing split (FEATURE_CATALOG.md vs feature_catalog.md) must be normalized to a single canonical name during migration or per-mode index files will collide/confuse. Per-mode subsections are the natural unit since structure is already parallel.

findings.area: Scenario / feature counts per skill (master-file self-declared)
findings.observation: Playbook scenario totals: deep-ai-council = 32 (9 categories, DAC- IDs), deep-context = 25 (7 categories), deep-research = 44 (8 categories: 35 DR- + 6 CP- + 3 fan-out), deep-review = 49 (9 categories: 33 dimension/lifecycle + 6 CP-052..057 + 6 DRV-058..063 + 4 DRV-064..067), deep-improvement = 48 (11 categories, COVERED_FEATURES==TOTAL_FEATURES==48). Feature-catalog category coverage counts also differ (e.g. deep-context catalog sums 3+4+3+4+3+3+5=25 features; deep-ai-council catalog declares 32 features/9 categories; deep-improvement catalog declares 6+3+4+4+6+1=24 catalog features mapped to 4 lanes).
findings.merge_implication: If merged into ONE playbook, the combined scenario total would be ~198 (32+25+44+49+48). The single master must either (a) keep per-mode sections each with its own count, or (b) compute one grand total. Every count phrase ('provides N deterministic scenarios across M categories') is hand-written prose that becomes stale the moment modes are concatenated — each is a manual edit point.

findings.observation: Self-checks are PROSE-embedded, not externally CI-enforced per-skill. deep-improvement playbook hard-codes 'COVERED_FEATURES == TOTAL_FEATURES == 48' (line 110) AND names exact closure-wave ID ranges (RT-022..031, CP-032..037, MB-038..042+MB-R01, SB-043..048, line 109). deep-ai-council uses the same Release Readiness clause but WITHOUT a baked number ('COVERED_FEATURES == TOTAL_FEATURES', line 127). deep-context/deep-research/deep-review state counts only in the OVERVIEW prose, not in a == N self-check. The generic system-spec-kit/scripts/tests/manual-playbook-runner.ts has NO per-skill hard-coded counts (grep for skill names = no matches), so it is path-driven, not a count guard.
findings.merge_implication: Merging forces the deep-improvement '== 48' literal to be rewritten to the new combined total (or restructured into a per-lane sub-total). The closure-wave ID list (RT-/CP-/MB-/SB-) is hand-maintained and lane-specific; it must be preserved verbatim or re-scoped under the merged mode section. These two literals are the highest-risk drift points in the merge.

findings.observation: Each skill uses a distinct, skill-specific scenario-ID prefix that is woven through BOTH the catalog and playbook and cross-referenced bidirectionally: deep-ai-council = DAC-NNN, deep-research = DR-NNN (+ CP-NNN stress + DR-05x fan-out), deep-review = CP-05x + DRV-05x..06x, deep-improvement = IS-/PG-/RT-/CP-/MB-/SB- (multi-lane prefixes), deep-context = (per-category IDs). Numbering RESTARTS per skill, so collisions exist across skills (e.g. CP-046..051 in deep-research vs CP-052..057 in deep-review vs CP-032..037 in deep-improvement; all three reuse the CP- prefix at different number bands).
findings.merge_implication: A single merged playbook CANNOT naively concatenate — the shared CP- prefix already overlaps across 3 skills at different number bands, and unifying them risks duplicate or ambiguous IDs. Migration must either (a) namespace IDs by mode (e.g. CTX-/RES-/REV-/IMP-/COU-) or (b) keep mode-scoped sub-playbooks under one skill folder. The bidirectional catalog<->playbook ID cross-refs mean any renumber touches both trees.

findings.area: SOURCE FILES traceability format (grep-checked path tables)
findings.observation: Per-feature catalog files carry a '## 3. SOURCE FILES' section with Implementation and Validation/Tests tables of literal repo paths, plus a '## 4. SOURCE METADATA' block naming the canonical catalog source and feature-file path, and an sk-doc template marker (<!-- sk-doc-template: skill_asset_feature_catalog -->). Paths are skill-rooted (e.g. .opencode/skills/deep-research/...) and point back to that skill's own playbook files as validation cross-refs.
findings.merge_implication: Every per-feature file's SOURCE FILES table embeds the OLD skill path (.opencode/skills/deep-research/...). If files physically move under deep-loop-workflows, every path in every SOURCE FILES + SOURCE METADATA block + Related-references link must be rewritten, or traceability greps break. This is the largest mechanical edit surface of the merge — ~198 per-feature files each with multiple skill-rooted paths.

findings.observation: deep-research's playbook master still declares (line 682, '## 15. FEATURE CATALOG CROSS-REFERENCE INDEX') that 'No dedicated feature_catalog/ package exists under .opencode/skills/deep-research/' — but a feature_catalog/ package DOES exist there now (feature_catalog.md + 01--loop-lifecycle/ etc.). The playbook also has a duplicate '## 15.' heading (AUTOMATED TEST CROSS-REFERENCE and FEATURE CATALOG CROSS-REFERENCE INDEX both numbered 15).
findings.evidence: .opencode/skills/deep-research/manual_testing_playbook/manual_testing_playbook.md:680-682 (claims no catalog) vs Glob showing .opencode/skills/deep-research/feature_catalog/feature_catalog.md exists
findings.merge_implication: Pre-existing intra-skill drift means the migration spec cannot assume the current docs are internally consistent. The merge is an opportunity to fix this stale claim, but it also signals these hand-maintained indexes drift silently — the phased spec should add a reconciliation/validation step (count + presence cross-check) rather than trusting the source prose.

findings.observation: Category groupings are per-skill and semantically different: deep-research/deep-review share a lifecycle taxonomy (entry-points, init, iteration, convergence, pause-resume, synthesis, command-flow, fan-out); deep-context uses a sweep-specific taxonomy (frontier-seeding, by-model-parallel-sweep, agreement-merge, convergence-detection, context-report-synthesis, coverage-graph-schema, runtime-robustness); deep-improvement uses a 4-LANE taxonomy (Lane A/B/C/D + Shared, 11 categories); deep-ai-council uses a council taxonomy (deliberation, seat-diversity, council-graph-integration, value-comparison). deep-improvement and deep-ai-council additionally carry lane/category-level scoping notes that gate which scenarios run.
findings.merge_implication: There is NO common category schema to fold into. A single flat catalog would lose the per-skill semantic grouping (especially deep-improvement's lane gating and deep-ai-council's council-graph categories). The realistic merge shape is per-mode top-level sections (Council / Context / Improvement / Research / Review), each preserving its own category subtree and lane notes — NOT a unified category list. This is the central design fork the spec must resolve.

findings.observation: Combined per-feature .md files across both governance trees are substantial: deep-ai-council ~32 catalog + ~32 playbook, deep-research ~17 catalog + 44 playbook, deep-review ~26 catalog + 49 playbook, deep-improvement ~9 catalog + 48 playbook, deep-context ~25 catalog + 25 playbook. Plus shared helper scripts inside playbook trees (e.g. 07--command-flow-stress-tests/setup-cp-sandbox.sh appears in deep-research, deep-review, and deep-improvement at 08--agent-discipline-stress-tests/).
findings.evidence: Glob of */{feature_catalog,manual_testing_playbook}/** (truncated listings); setup-cp-sandbox.sh present in deep-research, deep-review (07--command-flow-stress-tests/) and deep-improvement (08--agent-discipline-stress-tests/)
findings.merge_implication: The merge moves ~400+ markdown files plus duplicated sandbox shell scripts. Duplicated setup-cp-sandbox.sh across 3 skills could be deduplicated to one shared asset under the merged skill. Volume argues for a phased, mode-at-a-time migration (one mode's catalog+playbook per phase) rather than a big-bang move, so each phase's count self-check and SOURCE-FILES path rewrite can be validated independently.

open_questions: Is there an external CI/guard script (beyond the generic manual-playbook-runner) that asserts per-skill scenario counts or greps SOURCE FILES paths? A targeted check of system-spec-kit feature-catalog/playbook governance scripts and any pre-commit hook would confirm whether the '== 48' and count-prose drift is machine-caught or prose-only.

Does the existing deep-loop-runtime skill already carry any feature_catalog/ or manual_testing_playbook/ governance tree that the merged deep-loop-workflows would need to coexist with or inherit a naming convention from?

Are the feature-ID prefixes (DAC-, DR-, DRV-, IS-/PG-/MB-/SB-, CP-) referenced anywhere OUTSIDE these two trees (e.g. in spec folders, changelog, or test fixtures) such that a renumber/namespace during merge would ripple beyond the governance artifacts?

Should the merged skill keep deep-improvement's lane gating and deep-ai-council's council-graph categories as first-class sub-taxonomies, or flatten them — this is a design decision the spec must make, not mappable from current reality alone.

readers.slice: local scripts duplication across deep-context, deep-research, deep-review, deep-ai-council, deep-improvement (+ declared key_files ownership)

findings.observation: FOUR distinct reduce-state.cjs implementations exist: deep-context/scripts/reduce-state.cjs, deep-research/scripts/reduce-state.cjs, deep-review/scripts/reduce-state.cjs, and deep-improvement/scripts/shared/reduce-state.cjs. All four share a near-identical helper skeleton (readUtf8/writeUtf8/readJson, slugify, normalizeText, contentHash, writeTextAtomic) but each carries mode-specific reduce logic: context=agreement-weighted dedup by unit_id with KIND_TO_BUCKET + relevance gate (DEFAULT_RELEVANCE_GATE 0.55 / DEFAULT_AGREEMENT_MIN 2); review=P0/P1/P2 SEVERITY_WEIGHTS + REQUIRED_DIMENSIONS; research=resource-map emit + evidence synthesis; improvement=experiment registry/dashboard for two lanes. context+improvement both define byte-identical writeTextAtomic with the SAME 'Mirrors the runtime writeStateAtomic' comment.
findings.merge_implication: The reduce-state mode logic is genuinely mode-specific and belongs in deep-loop-workflows (per-mode reducer module), but the duplicated helper skeleton (atomic write, slugify, normalizeText, contentHash, JSON IO) is generic runtime logic. deep-loop-runtime already ships lib/deep-loop/atomic-state.ts (writeStateAtomic) which the inline writeTextAtomic copies in prose — a single merged skill should have ONE shared helper module (runtime) and N thin per-mode reducers (workflows), not 4 near-duplicate files.

findings.observation: deep-research/scripts/runtime-capabilities.cjs and deep-review/scripts/runtime-capabilities.cjs are byte-identical except TWO strings: the header banner ('Deep Research' vs 'Deep Review') and one error message ('Unknown deep-research runtime' vs 'Unknown deep-review runtime'). Same DEFAULT_CAPABILITY_PATH ('..','assets','runtime_capabilities.json'), same loadRuntimeCapabilities/listRuntimeCapabilityIds/resolveRuntimeCapability functions, same CLI entry, same exports. deep-context, deep-ai-council, and deep-improvement have NO runtime-capabilities.cjs.
findings.evidence: deep-research/scripts/runtime-capabilities.cjs:1-115; deep-review/scripts/runtime-capabilities.cjs:1-115 (differ only at lines 2-3 banner and line 71 error string)
findings.merge_implication: This is the single strongest pure-dedup candidate: a generic runtime-capability resolver duplicated verbatim. It is generic runtime logic (loads assets/runtime_capabilities.json by mode) and belongs in deep-loop-runtime as one parameterized resolver taking a mode/asset-path argument. Merge collapses 2 files to 1; the per-mode runtime_capabilities.json asset stays mode-scoped in workflows.

findings.area: loop-lock.cjs (already a runtime wrapper — the merge pattern)
findings.observation: deep-context/scripts/loop-lock.cjs is NOT a standalone implementation; it is a thin host-facing wrapper that tsx-requires deep-loop-runtime/lib/deep-loop/loop-lock.ts (the real implementation). Its header explicitly states it exists 'so the host-driven context loop gets single-writer advisory locking from the same source of truth the mature loops use.' deep-context is the only one of the five with a local loop-lock.cjs.
findings.merge_implication: This is the reference pattern the whole merge should follow: mode skill keeps a thin wrapper, runtime owns the logic. deep-loop-runtime ALREADY has loop-lock.ts, atomic-state.ts, jsonl-repair.ts, convergence.cjs, fanout-*.cjs, query/status/upsert.cjs, and coverage-graph/council libs — so the generic backend largely exists. The merge mostly needs to (a) move duplicated helper skeletons up to runtime and (b) keep thin per-mode reducers/wrappers in deep-loop-workflows.

findings.observation: deep-research and deep-review reduce-state.cjs import resolveArtifactRoot AND emitResourceMap from system-spec-kit (../../system-spec-kit/shared/review-research-paths.cjs and scripts/resource-map/extract-from-evidence.cjs); deep-context reduce-state.cjs imports resolveArtifactRoot from the same system-spec-kit path. resolveArtifactRoot is defined in system-spec-kit/shared/review-research-paths.cjs:202, NOT in deep-loop-runtime.
findings.merge_implication: A merged deep-loop-workflows skill inherits a cross-skill dependency on system-spec-kit/shared for path + resource-map helpers. The two-skill target architecture (runtime + workflows) does NOT fully isolate the backend: these shared helpers live in system-spec-kit, so the migration spec must decide whether resolveArtifactRoot/emitResourceMap stay in system-spec-kit (cross-skill import preserved) or move into deep-loop-runtime. Relative '../../' import paths will all shift when scripts move into one skill folder.

findings.area: verify-yaml-script-paths.sh (cross-cutting verifier in one mode's folder)
findings.observation: deep-research/scripts/verify-yaml-script-paths.sh verifies Node .cjs script paths for BOTH research AND review workflow YAMLs (its YAML_FILES array lists deep_start-research-loop_*.yaml AND deep_start-review-loop_*.yaml). It is a cross-mode verifier that physically lives only in deep-research/scripts/. No equivalent exists in deep-review.
findings.evidence: deep-research/scripts/verify-yaml-script-paths.sh:7-12 (YAML_FILES covers both research and review auto/confirm yamls)
findings.merge_implication: This is generic verification tooling misfiled under one mode. In the merged skill it naturally becomes ONE workflow-level verifier covering all modes' YAMLs (or moves to runtime). Confirms the current split has already leaked cross-mode generics into single-mode folders — the merge resolves the misplacement.

findings.observation: deep-ai-council/scripts/ is a self-contained mode package: orchestrate-session.cjs, orchestrate-topic.cjs, advise-council-completion.cjs, persist-artifacts.cjs, replay-graph-from-artifacts.cjs, plus a lib/ subtree (findings-registry.cjs, rollback.cjs, audit-trail.cjs, persist-artifacts.cjs, package.json) and a full tests/ suite with its own node_modules/.vite. Council backend primitives (multi-seat-dispatch, round-state-jsonl, session-state-hierarchy, adjudicator-verdict-scoring, council-graph-db, cost-guards) ALREADY live separately in deep-loop-runtime/lib/council/.
findings.evidence: Glob deep-ai-council/scripts/** (orchestrate-*.cjs, lib/findings-registry.cjs, lib/audit-trail.cjs, lib/rollback.cjs, lib/package.json, tests/*.vitest.ts, node_modules/.vite); deep-loop-runtime/lib/council/*.cjs
findings.merge_implication: deep-ai-council scripts are overwhelmingly mode-specific orchestration (belong in deep-loop-workflows) — its generic council backend was ALREADY extracted to deep-loop-runtime/lib/council/. Watch items for the migration: this folder has its own lib/package.json + node_modules/.vite (build/test isolation), and a findings-registry.cjs that may overlap conceptually with the reduce-state registries and the deep-improvement candidate registry — needs a dedup check during the spec, not assumed identical.

findings.area: deep-improvement scripts (large two-lane package, mostly mode-specific)
findings.observation: deep-improvement/scripts/ is by far the largest: a router (shared/loop-host.cjs), shared/ (reduce-state.cjs, improvement-journal.cjs, promote-candidate.cjs, mutation-coverage.cjs, materialize-benchmark-fixtures.cjs), two lanes agent-improvement/ and model-benchmark/ (incl. a scorer/ subtree with grader/deterministic/lib + git-ignored cache), lib/ helpers (typed-errors.cjs, promotion-gates.cjs, mirror-sync-verify.cjs), a skill-benchmark/ tree, plus vitest.config.mjs. Its README declares loop-host.cjs as the single router and lib/ as 'not a cross-skill import surface.'
findings.evidence: deep-improvement/scripts/README.md:16-22,84-107; Glob deep-improvement/scripts/** (shared/, agent-improvement/, model-benchmark/scorer/, skill-benchmark/, lib/typed-errors.cjs, lib/promotion-gates.cjs)
findings.merge_implication: Almost all of deep-improvement scripts are mode/lane-specific (agent-improvement, model-benchmark, skill-benchmark) and belong in deep-loop-workflows, but the merge must preserve its internal lane-router pattern (loop-host.cjs resolveScriptPath) and its own vitest.config.mjs. Generic candidates to watch: typed-errors.cjs and the writeTextAtomic in shared/reduce-state.cjs overlap conceptually with runtime helpers; promotion-gates.cjs is lane-specific. This skill, NOT the smaller three, sets the structural template (mode/lane subdirs + shared/ + lib/) the merged scripts/ tree will likely adopt.

findings.observation: Each skill's graph-metadata.json derived.key_files declares which scripts it owns: deep-context declares scripts/reduce-state.cjs; deep-research declares scripts/reduce-state.cjs; deep-review declares scripts/reduce-state.cjs; deep-ai-council declares scripts/persist-artifacts.cjs; deep-improvement declares scripts/agent-improvement/scan-integration.cjs, scripts/agent-improvement/score-candidate.cjs, scripts/shared/promote-candidate.cjs. NONE declares runtime-capabilities.cjs, loop-lock.cjs, or verify-yaml-script-paths.sh in key_files. Also: deep-context/research/review have family='deep-loop' + sibling edge to deep-loop-runtime (weight 0.6); deep-ai-council and deep-improvement have family='sk-util' with NO deep-loop-runtime sibling edge.
findings.evidence: deep-context/graph-metadata.json:95-106,26-29; deep-research/graph-metadata.json:87-97; deep-review/graph-metadata.json:101-112; deep-ai-council/graph-metadata.json:4,90-97 (no deep-loop-runtime edge); deep-improvement/graph-metadata.json:4,93-104 (sibling only sk-prompt)
findings.merge_implication: Two governance signals for the spec: (1) The duplicated generics (runtime-capabilities.cjs, loop-lock.cjs, verify-yaml-script-paths.sh) are UNDECLARED in any key_files — they are invisible to graph traversal and can be moved/dedup'd with low metadata churn. (2) The family split is real: deep-context/research/review already self-identify as 'deep-loop' family with a deep-loop-runtime edge, but deep-ai-council and deep-improvement are 'sk-util' with NO runtime edge despite already consuming runtime/lib/council. The merge must reconcile all five into one deep-loop-workflows skill_id, rewrite every key_files path to the new location, and add the deep-loop-runtime dependency edge for the two sk-util members.

open_questions: Are deep-ai-council/scripts/lib/findings-registry.cjs, the deep-improvement candidate/experiment registry, and the three reduce-state registries conceptually the same 'findings/units registry' abstraction (true dedup candidate) or genuinely different schemas? Needs a side-by-side content read, not just filename inference.

Should resolveArtifactRoot + emitResourceMap (currently in system-spec-kit/shared, imported by 3 reducers) move into deep-loop-runtime, or stay as a cross-skill system-spec-kit dependency the merged workflows skill imports? Affects whether the two-skill architecture is truly self-contained.

deep-improvement and deep-ai-council ship their own node_modules/.vite and (improvement) vitest.config.mjs — how do per-mode test suites consolidate under one merged scripts/ tree without a single monolithic vitest config that breaks lane isolation?

Do the inline writeTextAtomic copies (deep-context + deep-improvement) behave identically to deep-loop-runtime/lib/deep-loop/atomic-state.ts writeStateAtomic, or do they intentionally differ (markdown vs JSON-only)? deep-context's header claims the runtime helper 'cannot serialize' markdown — confirm before collapsing to one helper.

slice: Command surface (8 deep commands + YAML assets)

readers.slice: deep command routers (.opencode/commands/deep/*.md) — command→skill reference map for the 5-skill → deep-loop-workflows merge

findings.observation: The 8 deep command markdown routers are deliberately thin and carry almost NO skill-path references in their bodies. A grep for `.opencode/skills/(deep-*)` across all 8 .md files returns only 4 hits total: start-research-loop.md:89 (deep-research/references/protocol/spec_check_protocol.md), start-non-dev-ai-system-loop.md:36+79 (deep-improvement SKILL.md + scripts/shared/loop-host.cjs), start-skill-benchmark-loop.md:36+76 (deep-improvement SKILL.md + loop-host.cjs). The 4 thin routers (start-context-loop, start-review-loop, ask-ai-council, start-model-benchmark-loop, start-agent-improvement-loop) reference their skill ONLY through the owned YAML assets, not the .md body.
findings.merge_implication: The markdown-router repointing surface for the merge is SMALL and concentrated: only 4 literal skill-path strings in 3 files need rewriting (deep-research/ and deep-improvement/ → deep-loop-workflows/). The bulk of the repointing work is NOT in the .md routers — it is in the YAML assets (see separate finding).
findings.evidence: .opencode/commands/deep/start-research-loop.md:89, start-non-dev-ai-system-loop.md:36,79, start-skill-benchmark-loop.md:36,76 (grep -o across 8 *.md = 4 distinct skill-path hits)

findings.area: command→skill map (YAML asset layer — the real coupling)
findings.observation: The real command→skill binding lives in the owned YAML assets, NOT the markdown. A grep across the 12 *_auto/_confirm.yaml in commands/deep/assets/ returns 270 skill-path occurrences. Each command's YAML references (a) its own mode skill and (b) the shared deep-loop-runtime. Verified per-command anchors: context_auto.yaml → deep-context/SKILL.md:79, references/protocol/loop_protocol.md, references/convergence/convergence.md, assets/deep_context_config.json, assets/context_report_template.md, scripts/loop-lock.cjs, scripts/reduce-state.cjs + deep-loop-runtime scripts. research_auto.yaml → deep-research/SKILL.md:68, references/protocol/{loop_protocol,spec_check_protocol}.md, references/state/state_format.md, references/convergence/convergence.md, assets/deep_research_{config.json,strategy.md,dashboard.md,prompt_pack_iteration.md.tmpl}, scripts/reduce-state.cjs. review_auto.yaml → deep-review/SKILL.md:57 + parallel references/state/assets/scripts structure. council_auto.yaml → deep-ai-council/SKILL.md:41, scripts/orchestrate-session.cjs, scripts/lib/findings-registry.cjs, scripts/replay-graph-from-artifacts.cjs. agent-improvement_auto.yaml → deep-improvement/SKILL.md:81, assets/agent_improvement/*, scripts/agent-improvement/*, scripts/model-benchmark/run-benchmark.cjs, scripts/shared/*.
findings.merge_implication: The phased migration spec MUST treat the YAML assets as the primary repoint surface (~270 path strings across 12 files), not the .md routers. Every `.opencode/skills/<mode-skill>/...` path inside the YAML must repoint to `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-workflows/...` while every `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/...` path stays unchanged. The 18 asset files (12 yaml + 6 presentation.txt) travel WITH their command and are owned by commands/deep/, so they are migrated in place — only their internal skill-path strings change.
findings.evidence: grep -c over commands/deep/assets/*.yaml = 270 hits/12 files; per-command anchors: deep_start-context-loop_auto.yaml:79-92, deep_ask-ai-council_auto.yaml:41-52, deep_start-research-loop_auto.yaml:68-94, deep_start-review-loop_auto.yaml:57-64, deep_start-agent-improvement-loop_auto.yaml:81-94

findings.observation: The 5 mode skills share an identical internal layout that the YAML assets address by parallel paths: SKILL.md, references/protocol/loop_protocol.md, references/convergence/convergence.md, references/state/state_format.md (research/review), assets/<mode>_config.json, assets/<mode>_strategy.md, scripts/reduce-state.cjs, scripts/loop-lock.cjs (context). deep-ai-council and deep-improvement diverge: council uses scripts/orchestrate-session.cjs + scripts/lib/findings-registry.cjs; deep-improvement uses scripts/shared/loop-host.cjs as a single entrypoint plus mode-namespaced subtrees (scripts/agent-improvement/, scripts/model-benchmark/, assets/agent_improvement/, assets/model_benchmark/, references/skill_benchmark/, references/non_dev_ai_system/).
findings.merge_implication: Folding 5 skills into deep-loop-workflows will COLLIDE on shared filenames: 5 different SKILL.md, 4+ reduce-state.cjs, multiple convergence.md/loop_protocol.md/config.json/strategy.md. The merge must namespace per-mode (e.g. references/context/, references/research/, assets/research/, scripts/research/) — exactly the pattern deep-improvement already uses internally for its 4 lanes. The YAML path rewrites must encode this new namespacing, not a flat move.
findings.evidence: deep_start-research-loop_auto.yaml:68-76 vs deep_start-review-loop_auto.yaml:57-64 (parallel structure), deep_start-agent-improvement-loop_auto.yaml:83-94 (already mode-namespaced agent_improvement/ + model_benchmark/ subtrees)

findings.area: deep-improvement is already a 4-lane multi-mode skill
findings.observation: 4 of the 8 commands (start-agent-improvement-loop=Lane A, start-model-benchmark-loop=Lane B, start-skill-benchmark-loop=Lane C, start-non-dev-ai-system-loop=Lane D) ALL route into the single deep-improvement skill. Lanes C and D bypass the YAML/presentation pattern entirely: their .md routers directly invoke `node .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/scripts/shared/loop-host.cjs --mode=<skill-benchmark|non-dev-ai-system-refine>` (start-skill-benchmark-loop.md:76, start-non-dev-ai-system-loop.md:79) and declare `skill: deep-improvement` in frontmatter. Lanes A/B use the full YAML-asset pattern. start-agent-improvement-loop also cross-routes to start-model-benchmark-loop when lane=model-benchmark.
findings.merge_implication: deep-improvement is the proof-of-concept that one skill can host many command-facing modes via a shared loop-host.cjs + --mode dispatch — this is the architectural template for deep-loop-workflows. The merge can model context/research/review/council the same way (one skill, mode-dispatched). The 2 frontmatter `skill: deep-improvement` declarations (start-skill-benchmark-loop.md:3, start-non-dev-ai-system-loop.md:3) and the 2 direct loop-host.cjs invocations are explicit repoint targets → deep-loop-workflows.

findings.area: deep-loop-runtime references must NOT be touched
findings.observation: Every command's YAML already references the shared backend at `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/...` for cross-mode primitives: scripts/convergence.cjs, scripts/upsert.cjs, scripts/fanout-run.cjs, scripts/fanout-merge.cjs, scripts/status.cjs, lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts, lib/council/{multi-seat-dispatch,session-state-hierarchy,round-state-jsonl,cost-guards}.cjs, lib/deep-loop/{executor-config,executor-audit,prompt-pack,post-dispatch-validate}.ts. ask-ai-council.md:73 also names deep-loop-runtime CLI with `--loop-type council` in the .md body itself.
findings.merge_implication: The two-skill target architecture is ALREADY half-realized: deep-loop-runtime is the established shared backend and the YAML assets already split runtime-vs-mode cleanly. The merge's repoint rule is mechanically simple to scope: rewrite ONLY `deep-loop-runtime`-prefixed paths are UNCHANGED; rewrite the 5 mode-skill prefixes to `deep-loop-workflows`. ask-ai-council.md:73's `--loop-type council` runtime CLI reference is a body-level deep-loop-runtime mention that stays as-is.

findings.observation: All 8 commands share an identical Phase-0 @general agent verification HARD BLOCK (☐ BLOCKED self-check) followed by a BLOCKED setup/input gate. Two structural variants: (1) the 6 YAML-pattern commands (context, research, review, council, agent-improvement, model-benchmark) use 'PHASE 0 + MANDATORY INPUT GATE + MODE ROUTING + OWNED ASSETS table + PRESENTATION BOUNDARY + WORKFLOW SUMMARY' with per-command required-input lists (e.g. review needs review_target/review_target_type/review_dimensions; council needs deliberation_topic/max_rounds_per_topic/saturation_threshold/executor.*); (2) the 2 loop-host commands (skill-benchmark, non-dev-ai-system) use a lighter 'PHASE 0 + SETUP + RUN + OUTPUT + PRESENTATION BOUNDARY + SCOPE' shape with inline bash RUN blocks. Each command's failure block hardcodes its own restart hint (e.g. ask-ai-council.md:49 displays `/deep:ask-ai-council`).
findings.merge_implication: The Phase-0 gate text is per-command but structurally clonable — the merge does NOT need to touch Phase-0 verification logic (it references @general, not any deep-* skill). The setup gates name per-mode required-input contracts that must survive the merge intact, so the consolidated skill must preserve 5 distinct setup-input schemas (one per mode). No skill-path repoint is needed inside Phase-0/setup gates except the 2 loop-host RUN blocks and the 2 SKILL.md read-hints in skill-benchmark/non-dev-ai-system Phase-0.

findings.observation: 3 router WORKFLOW SUMMARY sections carry explicit sibling-isolation warnings baked into the command body: start-context-loop.md:114 ('do not transfer sibling defaults from deep-research or deep-review'), start-review-loop.md:113 ('review-specific convergenceThreshold severity-weighted finding ratio; do not transfer sibling defaults from deep-research or deep-ai-council'), ask-ai-council.md:126 ('council-specific 0.20 default on adjudicator-verdict stability; do not transfer sibling defaults from deep-review or deep-research'). Each mode has a distinct convergence semantic (context=relevance-gated coverage saturation; review=severity-weighted finding ratio; council=adjudicator-verdict stability).
findings.merge_implication: These are NOT skill-path references but they are load-bearing for the merge: folding 5 skills into one risks exactly the cross-default contamination these warnings guard against. The merged deep-loop-workflows skill must keep per-mode convergence configs strictly partitioned (the YAML already points each command at its own assets/<mode>_config.json). The spec should preserve these 'do not transfer sibling defaults' guardrails verbatim, now WITHIN one skill rather than across skills.

findings.area: allowed-tools / frontmatter drift across the 8 routers
findings.observation: The 8 routers have inconsistent frontmatter. The 4 YAML-pattern dev commands (context, research, review, council) declare a long allowed-tools list including the MCP code-graph + spec-memory tools; research additionally has WebFetch (start-research-loop.md:4). agent-improvement and model-benchmark declare a shorter `Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Task` with NO MCP tools. The 2 loop-host commands (skill-benchmark, non-dev-ai-system) have MINIMAL frontmatter — only `description` + `skill: deep-improvement`, no allowed-tools, no argument-hint. ask-ai-council uniquely has a `title:` field.
findings.merge_implication: The merge does not strictly require frontmatter changes for the skill repoint, but the spec should note that the 2 `skill: deep-improvement` frontmatter values (skill-benchmark, non-dev-ai-system) are the only frontmatter-level skill bindings and must repoint to `skill: deep-loop-workflows`. The other 6 commands bind to skills only via asset paths, so their frontmatter is untouched by the merge.
findings.evidence: start-research-loop.md:4 (WebFetch), start-agent-improvement-loop.md:4 (short list), start-skill-benchmark-loop.md:1-4 + start-non-dev-ai-system-loop.md:1-4 (minimal, skill: deep-improvement), ask-ai-council.md:2 (title:)

readers.slice: command YAML assets — /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/commands/deep/assets/ (read context_auto, research_auto, review_auto, agent-improvement_auto, ai-council_auto in full/partial; grep-verified all 12 *.yaml + 6 *.txt presentation files)

findings.observation: Every workflow YAML carries a top-level `skill_reference:` block with two literal strings pinning it to ONE of the 5 merging skills: `skill: <name>` and `skill_md: .opencode/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. Verified pairs: context_auto:78-79 + context_confirm:73-74 -> deep-context; research_auto:67-68 + research_confirm:53-54 -> deep-research; review_auto:56-57 + review_confirm:56-57 -> deep-review; agent-improvement_auto:80-81 + _confirm:86-87 -> deep-improvement; model-benchmark_auto:83-84 + _confirm:88-89 -> deep-improvement; ai-council_auto:40-41 + _confirm:40-41 -> deep-ai-council. Clean 1:1 per-skill mapping, no cross-references between the 5 skills.
findings.merge_implication: Mechanical, deterministic rewrite target. After merge each `skill_md` must repoint to `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-workflows/SKILL.md` and `skill:` to `deep-loop-workflows`; the per-mode identity currently carried by the skill name must move into a mode/lane field (loopType already exists in config). 12 YAML files x 2 strings = 24 literal edits, plus deeper reference sub-keys (next finding).

findings.observation: Beyond skill_md, each YAML's skill_reference embeds MANY more literal `.opencode/skills/<merging-skill>/...` paths to mode-private references, templates, assets, and scripts. deep-context (auto:80-93): references/protocol/loop_protocol.md, references/convergence/convergence.md, assets/deep_context_config.json, assets/context_report_template.md. deep-research (auto:69-76): 4 reference docs + assets/deep_research_config.json + deep_research_strategy.md. deep-review (auto:58-64): 3 reference docs + 2 assets. deep-improvement (auto:82-94): 4 assets/agent_improvement/* + 7 scripts under scripts/agent-improvement|model-benchmark|shared/. deep-ai-council (auto:42-45): scripts/orchestrate-session.cjs, lib/findings-registry.cjs, replay-graph-from-artifacts.cjs. grep counted 226 raw `.opencode/skills/<5skills>/` occurrences across 18 files (highest: agent-improvement 30/file, research 14, context 13, review 11).
findings.merge_implication: The merge must physically relocate each mode's references/, assets/, and scripts/ subtrees under deep-loop-workflows/ AND rewrite every literal path. This is the bulk of the migration cost (~226 path strings in commands alone, before counting the skill bodies). Naming collisions are guaranteed: every mode has its own reduce-state.cjs, deep_*_config.json, convergence.md, loop_protocol.md, strategy.md — a flat merge would clobber. The new skill needs per-mode subdirectories (e.g. deep-loop-workflows/modes/<mode>/) or the literal paths stay mode-namespaced.

findings.observation: Each loop YAML dispatches its native seat by a bare same-named agent. agent_config block: `agent: deep-context|deep-research|deep-review` + `agent_file: .opencode/agents/<name>.md` (context_auto:98-100, research_auto:81-83, review_auto:69-72). Inside the loop, `dispatch: agent: <name>` recurs (e.g. context_auto:394 step_sweep_native_batch, research_auto:170/621, review_auto:160/762). The agent name string equals the skill name for context/research/review. deep-improvement and deep-ai-council do NOT declare an agent_config / native @deep-X seat in these YAMLs — improvement dispatches via scripts (scan-integration.cjs etc.) and council via orchestrate-session.cjs; their work is script-orchestrated, not Task-agent-dispatched.
findings.merge_implication: Two distinct dispatch shapes survive the merge: (a) context/research/review fan out to a native Task subagent named identically to the old skill — these agent names + .opencode/agents/<name>.md files are a SEPARATE merge axis (per memory deep-loop-agent-runtime-mirrors, each is 3 runtime files). (b) improvement/council are pure-script orchestration with no native deep-X agent. A single deep-loop-workflows skill cannot collapse the agents trivially: either keep 3 mode-named agents or introduce one parameterized @deep-loop agent. The YAML `agent:` value is the load-bearing dispatch key and must stay resolvable to a real agent file.

findings.observation: CLI executor config is NOT duplicated per-skill in these YAMLs — it is delegated to deep-loop-runtime. research_auto resolves via `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/lib/deep-loop/executor-config.ts#parseExecutorConfig` (:584) and branches if_cli_codex/if_cli_claude_code/if_cli_opencode (:625-752). cli-opencode invocation: `opencode run --model {config.executor.model} --format json --dangerously-skip-permissions --pure --dir {repo_root} {optional_variant_flag} ... </dev/null` where optional_variant_flag expands to `--variant {config.executor.reasoningEffort}` (research_auto:729-752). context_auto routes CLI seats through `deep-loop-runtime/lib/council/multi-seat-dispatch.cjs#dispatchCouncilSeats` and reuses `scripts/fanout-run.cjs#buildLineageCommand` (:412-456). council declares executor inline (mode in-cli|external-cli, cli active-runtime|cli-codex|cli-claude-code|cli-opencode, model, reasoning) at ai-council_auto:24-28. model/variant are config-driven ({config.executor.model}, reasoningEffort->--variant), never hardcoded.
findings.merge_implication: Executor/CLI plumbing already lives in deep-loop-runtime (the intended shared backend) and is referenced, not redefined, by the command YAMLs. This axis is LOW merge cost — the literal `deep-loop-runtime/...` paths are stable and correctly point to the surviving shared skill. The merge does NOT need to touch CLI dispatch shape; it only needs to ensure the merged skill keeps using the same runtime helpers. council's inline executor schema differs from research/context's config.executor shape — a unified skill should reconcile these two executor-config conventions.

findings.area: runtime backend already split out (deep-loop-runtime references)
findings.observation: All 5 modes already reference the shared deep-loop-runtime backend via literal `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/...` paths, confirming the two-skill target is partially realized. context_auto runtime block (:86-92): coverage-graph-db.ts, convergence.cjs, upsert.cjs, fanout-run.cjs, fanout-merge.cjs, council/multi-seat-dispatch.cjs. research/review call convergence.cjs/upsert.cjs/fanout-run.cjs/fanout-merge.cjs and lib/deep-loop/{executor-config,executor-audit,prompt-pack,post-dispatch-validate}.ts. ai-council runtime_primitives (:46-52): lib/council/{session-state-hierarchy,round-state-jsonl,cost-guards}.cjs + upsert/status/convergence.cjs --loop-type council. By contrast, reduce-state.cjs is mode-LOCAL: each skill ships its own (`deep-context/scripts/reduce-state.cjs` context_auto:521; `deep-research/scripts/reduce-state.cjs` research_auto:779; deep-improvement/scripts/shared/reduce-state.cjs improvement_auto:94). loop-lock.cjs is also mode-local (deep-context/scripts/loop-lock.cjs, context_auto:174).
findings.merge_implication: The backend/frontend boundary is already drawn: shared mechanics (convergence, upsert, fanout, council dispatch, executor config/audit, coverage-graph, prompt-pack) live in deep-loop-runtime and stay put. What moves into deep-loop-workflows is the per-mode reduce-state.cjs, loop-lock.cjs, config/strategy/report templates, reference docs, and the loop YAML semantics. Note `--loop-type <mode>` is the existing parameter that already distinguishes modes inside shared runtime calls (context/research/review/council) — this is the natural seam for a parameterized merged skill. reduce-state.cjs divergence per mode is a real obstacle: 5 mode-specific reducers must coexist (subdir or dispatch-by-loopType).

findings.area: mode-shape divergence: loop vs session vs evaluation
findings.observation: The 5 YAMLs are NOT uniform loop instances. context/research/review share the phase_init -> phase_loop (read_state/converge/dispatch/reduce) -> phase_synthesis -> phase_save skeleton with near-identical step names and a STOP_REASONS enum [converged, maxIterationsReached, blockedStop, stuckRecovery, error, manualStop, userPaused] (context_auto:591, research_auto:556). But: deep-improvement is an `iterative_evaluation_loop` with scan->propose->score(5-dim)->benchmark->reduce->decide and guarded promotion (improvement_auto:11-24,117) — different phases, script-driven, plus a separate model-benchmark variant reusing the same skill. deep-ai-council is an `iterative_multi_topic_loop` (session->topic->round) fully delegated to orchestrate-session.cjs (ai-council_auto:5,93-109) — no per-iteration agent dispatch, no convergence.cjs inline vote. context uses by-model-shared-scope parallel-sweep fan-out (host-writes-state, read-only seats); research/review use autonomous-lineage fan-out (fanout-run.cjs spawns full-loop subprocesses).
findings.merge_implication: A single skill must host at least 3 structurally different workflow shapes (loop, evaluation, multi-topic-session) plus 2 fan-out paradigms (by-model-shared-scope vs autonomous-lineage). The merge cannot be a single linear workflow doc — deep-loop-workflows needs a mode dispatcher (loopType -> {context|research|review|improvement|council}) selecting the right phase graph, reducer, fan-out mode, and (for context/research/review) native agent. The command YAML assets themselves likely stay as 12 separate files (one per command x mode); only their skill_reference/path strings repoint. The shared invariants (STOP_REASONS enum, host-writes-state, lock-cleanup contract, predicate grammar) are merge-friendly common ground to centralize.

findings.observation: The assets dir holds 12 workflow YAMLs (auto+confirm for each of context, research, review, agent-improvement, model-benchmark, ai-council) + 6 presentation .txt files (one per command family). model-benchmark is a SIXTH command surface that also binds to deep-improvement (skill_md model-benchmark_auto:84). So the 5 merging skills back 6 command families / 12 YAMLs / 6 presentation files = 18 asset files total referencing the merge set (grep: 226 path occurrences across 18 files; presentation .txt files carry 2-13 occurrences each).
findings.merge_implication: Migration spec scope for THIS slice = 18 files, not 10. The agent-improvement and model-benchmark commands both collapse onto deep-improvement, so the merged skill must serve two command families from one mode. Presentation .txt files also carry literal skill paths and must be included in the rewrite sweep (easy to miss — they are not .yaml). A phased migration can rewrite path strings mechanically (deterministic find/replace per file) but must preserve the per-command/per-mode YAML separation since each is a distinct command entrypoint.

findings.merge_implication: Presentation .txt files need a SECOND class of edit beyond path renames: SKILL.md pointers move to deep-loop-workflows/SKILL.md (likely with a mode/section anchor), and cross-skill prose like 'see deep-review skill' becomes intra-skill 'see deep-review MODE'. memory_context seed strings (e.g. `input: "deep-research"` at research presentation L129, `input: "deep-ai-council"` at ask-ai-council L121) are search seeds that may need re-seeding to the new skill name. The migration spec should explicitly EXCLUDE descriptions.json from the rename scope to avoid corrupting historical spec-folder records. This narrows the descriptions half of the slice to zero work.

findings.area: non-deep commands — speckit references are AGENT refs, not skill refs

readers.slice: native agent mirrors (.opencode canonical + .claude + .codex for {deep-context, deep-research, deep-review, deep-improvement, ai-council})

findings.observation: All 5 deep agents have a complete 3-runtime mirror set (15 files total), confirmed via Glob: .opencode/agents/<name>.md (canonical) + .claude/agents/<name>.md + .codex/agents/<name>.toml for each of {deep-context, deep-research, deep-review, deep-improvement, ai-council}. No mirror is missing.
findings.merge_implication: If the 5 skills merge but agents stay separate, all 15 files persist as-is. If agents also consolidate/rename, every change must be applied 3x per agent (canonical + 2 mirrors) or a mirror goes stale (per the deep-loop-agent-runtime-mirrors convention: a missing/stale mirror = silent native-seat failure for that runtime).

findings.observation: Agent `name:` frontmatter values are {deep-context, deep-research, deep-review, deep-improvement, ai-council}. Four match their owning skill 1:1, but the ai-council agent is named `ai-council` while its owning skill is `deep-ai-council` — a pre-existing name asymmetry. The agent writes `ai-council/**` artifacts and is dispatched by `/deep:ask-ai-council`.
findings.evidence: .opencode/agents/ai-council.md:2 (name: ai-council) vs skill path .opencode/skills/deep-ai-council referenced at ai-council.md:37,424,646,...
findings.merge_implication: When the 5 skills become one `deep-loop-workflows` skill, the agents do NOT need to rename — agent names are independent of the skill name and are dispatched by-name by loop YAML per runtime. The existing ai-council/deep-ai-council mismatch is precedent that skill-rename does not force agent-rename. Agent renames would be a separate, higher-risk change (loop YAML dispatches seats by name).

findings.observation: deep-research.md hard-references its own skill ONCE for a doc pointer: `.opencode/skills/deep-research/references/protocol/loop_protocol.md` (lifecycle branches). It does NOT `Read()` a SKILL.md at runtime; the reference is a doc citation. All other state references are packet-local files (research/deep-research-*.{json,jsonl,md}), not skill paths.
findings.merge_implication: On merge, this single skill-path citation must repoint from `.opencode/skills/deep-research/...` to the new `deep-loop-workflows/...` location (wherever the deep-research mode's loop_protocol doc lands). One repoint per runtime file = 3 edits for this agent.

findings.observation: ai-council.md is by far the most skill-path-coupled: 9+ distinct `.opencode/skills/deep-ai-council/...` references plus 1 `.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/...`. They include the persist-artifacts library path (`scripts/lib/persist-artifacts.cjs`), the CLI helper (`scripts/persist-artifacts.cjs`), output_schema.md, folder_layout.md, state_format.md (cited twice), command_wiring.md, and the SKILL.md Deep Mode section. The agent EXECUTES code from these paths (named exports of persist-artifacts.cjs), so they are load-bearing, not just citations.
findings.merge_implication: ai-council is the highest-effort agent to repoint. Every `.opencode/skills/deep-ai-council/...` path (including the executable persist-artifacts.cjs script paths) must move to the merged `deep-loop-workflows/` layout AND the agent body updated in all 3 runtime files. The output_schema.md citation explicitly requires lockstep update across the agent section, persist-artifacts.cjs, and the schema doc — confirm that lockstep contract survives the move. ~9 path edits x 3 runtimes = ~27 edits for ai-council alone.

findings.observation: deep-review.md references TWO skills: its own `.opencode/skills/deep-review/...` (loop_protocol.md lifecycle contract + the reducer script `scripts/reduce-state.cjs`) AND a SHARED, non-deep skill `.opencode/skills/sk-code-review/references/review_core.md` which it loads at runtime for severity definitions. The sk-code-review dependency is external to the 5-skill merge set.
findings.merge_implication: On merge, the `deep-review` self-paths repoint into `deep-loop-workflows/`, but the `sk-code-review` reference must NOT be touched — it points outside the merge set and stays valid. The migration spec must distinguish in-set skill paths (repoint) from out-of-set shared-skill paths (leave). Also note deep-review.md has an explicit 'Runtime Mirror Awareness' table (lines 288-291) naming its own .claude/.codex mirrors as read-only downstream surfaces — that self-referential table also repoints by content if agents rename.

findings.area: SKILL-LOAD references — deep-context (caller-table only, no Read() loads)
findings.observation: deep-context.md carries NO `.opencode/skills/...` path references at all. Its skill coupling is purely a 'Caller + Command Integrations' table naming skill NAMES as strings: `deep-context` (Loop skill), `deep-loop-runtime` (Runtime primitives), `system-code-graph`, `sk-prompt-small-model`, plus `/deep:start-context-loop`. These are documentation/routing strings, not runtime loads. Confirmed identical in the .claude mirror (table at lines 179-181 vs canonical 197-200).
findings.merge_implication: deep-context is the LOWEST-effort agent to migrate: only the bare skill-NAME string `deep-context` in its caller table changes to the new mode label (e.g. `deep-loop-workflows` context mode). `deep-loop-runtime`, `system-code-graph`, `sk-prompt-small-model` references all stay (runtime + out-of-set skills are unchanged by the merge). 1 string edit x 3 runtimes.

findings.area: SKILL-LOAD references — deep-improvement (skill-NAME strings, four-lane complexity)
findings.observation: deep-improvement.md has NO `.opencode/skills/...` path loads; its skill coupling is a Skills table naming `deep-improvement`, `sk-doc`, `system-spec-kit` as strings, plus extensive command/script references in a 'Lane awareness' paragraph (4 co-equal lanes A/B/C/D each with its own `/deep:start-*-loop` command and `scripts/...loop-host.cjs --mode=...` invocations). The lane scripts live under the deep-improvement skill tree but are referenced as relative `scripts/...` paths, not absolute skill paths.
findings.merge_implication: deep-improvement carries the most COMMAND-surface breadth (4 lanes, 4 distinct start-loop commands). The bare skill-name string `deep-improvement` in the Skills table repoints to the new mode label; `sk-doc`/`system-spec-kit` stay. The 4-lane awareness paragraph and its `scripts/.../loop-host.cjs --mode=*` references must be re-verified against wherever those scripts land in the merged skill — if scripts move, the relative-path narrative needs updating. Highest command-surface coordination risk of the five.

findings.observation: The .codex/*.toml mirrors wrap the FULL canonical markdown body verbatim inside `developer_instructions = '''...'''`, prefixed by a `# Agent: <name>` + `# Converted from: .opencode/agents/<name>.md` header and TOML metadata fields (`name`, `description`, `sandbox_mode`, `model`, `model_reasoning_effort`). Skill-load references are preserved character-identical inside the body (e.g. deep-research.toml:94 = canonical deep-research.md:107). The .codex mirror adds runtime-specific fields the .md files lack (e.g. deep-review.toml: model='gpt-5.4', model_reasoning_effort='high', sandbox_mode='workspace-write').
findings.merge_implication: Any skill-path repoint must be applied INSIDE the .toml developer_instructions triple-quoted body, not just to the .md. The `# Converted from:` header implies a conversion pipeline may regenerate .toml from canonical .md — confirm whether .toml is hand-edited or generated before the migration, so repoints aren't overwritten. The extra TOML model/sandbox fields are runtime config that must be preserved through any regeneration.

findings.observation: The single intentional divergence across the three mirrors is the 'Path Convention' line: canonical says 'Use only `.opencode/agents/*.md`', the .claude mirror says '.claude/agents/*.md', the .codex mirror says '.codex/agents/*.toml'. Everything else (skill-load refs, caller tables, contracts) is body-identical across all three.
findings.merge_implication: Parity verification for the migration must treat the Path Convention line as an EXPECTED diff, not drift. A naive byte-diff parity check across mirrors will always flag this one line — the migration spec/validation needs to whitelist it so genuine repoint drift (skill paths) isn't masked by noise.

findings.area: Self-referential mirror awareness tables (content that names the agents)
findings.observation: deep-review.md embeds a 'Runtime Mirror Awareness' table (lines 288-291) that literally names `.claude/agents/deep-review.md` and `.codex/agents/deep-review.toml` as its own downstream mirrors, and a caller table naming `/deep:start-review-loop`, the two auto/confirm YAML asset files, and sibling agents `@deep-research`, `@review`, `@orchestrate`. ai-council similarly self-references `/deep:ask-ai-council`.
findings.merge_implication: If any agent renames during the merge, these self-referential tables must update their own file-path and command-name strings in all 3 runtimes (a mirror naming its own siblings). If agents do NOT rename, these tables only need command/YAML-path repoints where the merged skill relocates the start-loop commands or their auto/confirm YAML assets. Cross-agent sibling references (@deep-research etc.) must stay consistent with whatever final agent-name set is chosen.

findings.observation: Multiple agents document SIBLING deep-loop thresholds inline as awareness notes: deep-research.md and ai-council.md both list per-skill convergence defaults (deep-review 0.10, deep-research 0.05, deep-ai-council 0.20) with a warning that thresholds are local to each skill's convergence semantics. These reference the OTHER skills by name in prose.
findings.merge_implication: After merge into one skill with multiple modes, these inline sibling-threshold notes ('deep-review uses X', 'deep-ai-council uses Y') describe what become co-resident MODES of one skill rather than separate skills. The prose framing ('Carrying threshold expectations across siblings') should be revisited so it reads as cross-MODE rather than cross-SKILL — a content (not path) edit, but one that touches the same convergence-semantics claim in 2+ agents x 3 runtimes.

open_questions: Is the .codex/*.toml mirror hand-edited or machine-generated from canonical .md (the '# Converted from:' header suggests a conversion pipeline)? This determines whether skill-path repoints in .toml survive or get overwritten — out of slice scope but critical for migration sequencing.

Does the loop dispatch YAML (under .opencode/commands/deep/assets/) reference these agents by `name:` frontmatter string, and would renaming any agent require coordinated YAML edits? Confirmed deep-review names its auto/confirm YAMLs but the YAML->agent name binding was not read in this slice.

Are the ai-council persist-artifacts.cjs script paths (.opencode/skills/deep-ai-council/scripts/lib/persist-artifacts.cjs) executed via the agent body's literal string, or resolved by the host? Confirm whether the operator intends agents to keep their current names (recommended by the existing ai-council/deep-ai-council asymmetry) or also rename to a unified scheme — this single decision changes migration effort from ~repoint-only (low) to ~rename+repoint+sibling-table-sync (high) across all 15 files.

findings.merge_implication: This README is written around a FIVE-consumer model. The per-consumer import table (L119-123) and the 'why council lives here vs deep-ai-council' rationale (L160-162) need to be re-anchored to the merged skill's mode names. findings.observation: `system-skill-advisor/mcp_server/lib/scorer/aliases.ts` hard-codes canonical alias groups for FOUR of the five: 'deep-research' (L13-19), 'deep-review' (L20-26), 'deep-improvement' (L27-32), 'deep-ai-council' (L39-47). findings.merge_implication: Merge must decide whether the 4 canonical alias keys collapse to one `deep-loop-workflows` key (losing per-mode routing granularity) or whether mode-specific aliases are retained pointing at the merged skill. findings.observation: The skill names appear across multiple advisor runtime/test artifacts: skill-graph.json (single minified line with the routing graph), scorer lib lexical.ts/explicit.ts/fusion.ts, and test fixtures (intent-prompt-corpus.ts, harder-intent-prompt-corpus.ts, routing-parity-deep-skills.vitest.ts, routing-parity-deep-council.vitest.ts, skill-advisor-cli-parity.vitest.ts, native-scorer.vitest.ts). findings.merge_implication: Beyond docs, the merge has a routing-graph + test-fixture blast radius in system-skill-advisor. graph-metadata.json edges, skill-graph.json nodes, and every routing-parity test that asserts a prompt resolves to e.g. `deep-review` must be updated to the merged skill key (or the alias layer must preserve the old keys). These tests will fail-closed if names change without fixture updates — a phased migration must sequence advisor changes with their test updates.

findings.observation: `system-spec-kit/constitutional/deep-skill-workflow-required.md` enumerates all 5 skill names as a YAML list (L9-13: deep-context, deep-research, deep-review, deep-ai-council, deep-improvement), references 'deep-context bypass' (L34), and lists the 4 loop-executor agents dispatched only by parent commands (L52).
