readers.slice: skill-advisor graph registration for the 5 deep-loop skills (deep-ai-council, deep-context, deep-improvement, deep-research, deep-review)

findings.observation: skill_id is HARD-bound to the folder name. parseSkillMetadata() throws 'skill_id does not match folder name' when graph-metadata.json skill_id != basename(dirname(sourcePath)). The runtime graph (skill-graph.sqlite) is built by indexSkillMetadata(skillDir) which discovers graph-metadata.json files per skill folder. Each of the 5 skills currently has skill_id == folder name (deep-ai-council, deep-context, deep-improvement, deep-research, deep-review), all schema_version 2.
findings.merge_implication: The merged skill MUST live at folder .opencode/skills/deep-loop-workflows/ with graph-metadata.json skill_id='deep-loop-workflows', or the scan throws and the whole graph rebuild fails. You cannot register 'deep-loop-workflows' metadata inside any of the 5 old folders. Migration = delete the 5 folders' metadata, create one new folder + metadata. Re-registration is a folder-level operation, not an in-place rename.

findings.observation: Node rows that disappear from the source dir are deleted first, and skill_edges has 'ON DELETE CASCADE' foreign keys on both source_id and target_id referencing skill_nodes(id). So deleting the 5 skill folders + rescanning auto-removes the 5 nodes AND every edge touching them (both outbound and inbound) via cascade. There is no manual edge-cleanup step required for the deleted nodes themselves.
findings.evidence: skill-graph-db.ts:218 (skill_docs ON DELETE CASCADE), :192 (target_id ON DELETE CASCADE), :839-841 (deleted nodes -> FK cascade removes stale edges); indexSkillMetadata deletes vanished nodes first
findings.merge_implication: Removing the 5 nodes is mechanically clean: delete folders, run skill_graph_scan, cascade handles edge teardown. The migration spec does NOT need a separate 'delete orphaned edges' phase for the 5 deleted nodes — the cascade covers it. The real work is repointing edges that SURVIVING skills still declare toward the 5 (those are re-published from the surviving source metadata on every scan, see UNKNOWN-TARGET finding).

findings.observation: On each scan every source edge is re-published; an edge whose target is not in knownSkillIds is REJECTED with a logged 'UNKNOWN-TARGET' warning and counted in rejectedEdges (not inserted, not fatal). So after the 5 nodes vanish, any surviving skill that still declares edges.* target='deep-context' etc. will silently drop those edges + emit warnings until its graph-metadata.json is repointed to 'deep-loop-workflows'.
findings.evidence: skill-graph-db.ts:976-1002 (re-publish loop; :992-997 UNKNOWN-TARGET rejection + warning + rejectedEdges++); :873-875 duplicate skill_id throws
findings.merge_implication: Every surviving skill that names one of the 5 in its edges must be edited and repointed to deep-loop-workflows in the SAME migration phase, else those relationships go dark. This is the core 'repoint reciprocal edges from siblings' task. It is silent-degradation (warnings only, no crash), so the spec needs an explicit verification step grepping rejectedEdges/UNKNOWN-TARGET after the scan.

findings.observation: Grep of all graph-metadata.json for edges targeting the 5 finds 5 SURVIVING skills with inbound edges that must be repointed: (1) system-skill-advisor 'enhances' deep-research/deep-review/deep-improvement/deep-ai-council (4 routing edges @0.7, one auto-added); (2) sk-code-review -> deep-review; (3) sk-prompt -> deep-improvement; (4) system-spec-kit -> deep-ai-council; (5) deep-loop-runtime 'enhances'+'siblings' deep-context/deep-review/deep-research (6 edges @0.7/0.6) + manual.related_to deep-review/deep-research/deep-ai-council.
findings.evidence: Grep '"target": "deep-(ai-council|context|improvement|research|review)"' over **/graph-metadata.json -> 9 files (4 are the merge targets themselves + system-skill-advisor); system-skill-advisor/graph-metadata.json:44-90; deep-loop-runtime/graph-metadata.json:16-49
findings.merge_implication: The repoint list is finite and known: 5 surviving skills. system-skill-advisor is the highest-stakes (it is the router that recommends these skills by name at Gate 2 — 4-5 edges collapse to 1 deep-loop-workflows edge). deep-loop-runtime currently 'enhances' 3 of the 5 by name; post-merge those 6+ edges collapse to a single deep-loop-runtime<->deep-loop-workflows pair. The spec must enumerate these 5 source files as required edits, and decide weight/context for the collapsed edges.

findings.observation: The 5 skills heavily cross-reference EACH OTHER: deep-context siblings deep-research/deep-review/deep-ai-council/deep-loop-runtime; deep-research siblings deep-context/deep-review/deep-ai-council/deep-loop-runtime; deep-review siblings deep-context/deep-research/deep-loop-runtime; deep-ai-council siblings deep-research/deep-context; deep-improvement siblings sk-prompt + manual.related_to deep-ai-council. All inter-target edges vanish automatically when the source nodes are deleted (they live in the deleted skills' own metadata).
findings.merge_implication: Roughly 15+ intra-cluster sibling edges need NO repointing — they disappear with their source nodes. The merged deep-loop-workflows skill needs only ONE outbound edge to deep-loop-runtime (the surviving backend) plus whatever cross-family edges it keeps (system-spec-kit, sk-code-review for review lane, sk-prompt/system-spec-kit for improvement lane, sk-code/system-code-graph for context lane). The new metadata's edge set is a UNION-and-dedup of the 5 old skills' external (non-intra-cluster) edges.

findings.observation: Each of the 5 SKILL.md carries a single HTML-comment Keywords line, and each graph-metadata.json carries derived.trigger_phrases + intent_signals. Keyword counts to merge: deep-context 12 keywords / 12 trigger_phrases; deep-ai-council 8 kw / 11 tp; deep-improvement 7 kw / 16 tp; deep-research 10 kw / 12 tp; deep-review 13 kw / 12 tp. Several trigger_phrases are duplicated within a single skill (e.g. deep-research has 'deep-research' twice; deep-improvement has 'deep-improvement' twice).
findings.evidence: deep-context/SKILL.md:8 (Keywords); deep-ai-council/SKILL.md:8; deep-improvement/SKILL.md:20; deep-research/SKILL.md:9; deep-review/SKILL.md:10; trigger_phrases in each graph-metadata.json derived block (e.g. deep-research:56-69 has 'deep-research' at :63 and :67)
findings.merge_implication: The merged skill's single Keywords line + trigger_phrases must UNION all 50 keywords / 63 trigger_phrases across the 5 (deduping existing intra-skill dupes), so Gate 2 advisor scoring still fires on 'deep research', 'context loop', 'deep review', 'ai council', 'improve agent', etc. If keyword union is incomplete, the advisor will under-route to deep-loop-workflows for whichever mode's phrases were dropped. This is the single biggest routing-quality risk of the merge — recommend a per-mode keyword-coverage checklist in the spec.

findings.observation: The 5 skills are NOT in one family/category today: deep-context/deep-research/deep-review are family='deep-loop' category='autonomous-loop'; deep-ai-council is family='sk-util' category='utility'; deep-improvement is family='sk-util' category='utility'. family must be in ALLOWED_FAMILIES or the scan throws. deep-loop-runtime is family='deep-loop' category='system'.
findings.evidence: deep-context/research/review graph-metadata.json:4-5 (deep-loop/autonomous-loop); deep-ai-council:4-5 (sk-util/utility); deep-improvement:4-5 (sk-util/utility); ALLOWED_FAMILIES check at skill-graph-db.ts:660-663
findings.merge_implication: The merged deep-loop-workflows must pick ONE family (logically 'deep-loop') and ONE category — collapsing 2 sk-util skills into the deep-loop family. Must verify 'deep-loop' is in ALLOWED_FAMILIES before writing the metadata (it is used by deep-loop-runtime, so almost certainly allowed). The domains/intent_signals arrays must union all 5 skills' domains (planning, deliberation, context-gathering, research, review, agent-improvement, benchmarking) so the advisor's domain-match scoring still covers every mode.

findings.observation: Each graph-metadata.json derived.key_files + derived.entities points at that skill's SKILL.md, README, references, assets, scripts, and BOTH runtime agent mirrors (.opencode/agents + .claude/agents). deep-improvement and deep-research/deep-review list a .claude/agents mirror; deep-context and deep-ai-council list only .opencode/agents. Total key_files across the 5: ~45 paths.
findings.evidence: deep-context/graph-metadata.json:95-106 (10 key_files incl. .opencode/agents/deep-context.md); deep-improvement:93-105 (incl. .claude/agents/deep-improvement.md AND .opencode/agents/deep-improvement.md); deep-review:101-112 (incl. review_mode_contract.yaml + both agent mirrors)
findings.merge_implication: The merged metadata's key_files/entities must be rebuilt to point at the new deep-loop-workflows/** paths (post-file-migration), NOT the old per-skill paths, or graph traversal and advisor file-context surfacing will reference dead paths. This is downstream of the actual file move — the spec should sequence: move files -> regenerate key_files/entities via generate-context.js/backfill -> scan. Note the per-skill agent-mirror asymmetry (.claude vs .opencode) is a pre-existing inconsistency the merge can normalize.

open_questions: Is 'deep-loop' an allowed family for a consumer/workflow skill, or is it reserved (deep-loop-runtime uses it with category='system')? Need to read ALLOWED_FAMILIES constant value at skill-graph-db.ts:~140 to confirm before authoring deep-loop-workflows metadata.

Does the merged skill keep 5 separate trigger-phrase namespaces or collapse to one ranked set? The advisor scorer's behavior with 63 unioned trigger_phrases on a single node (precision/recall impact) is not mapped in this slice.
Will the file migration use generate-context.js backfill to regenerate derived.key_files/entities, or be hand-authored? Determines whether key_files repointing is automatic or a manual spec task.

Are there edges or trigger references to the 5 skills OUTSIDE graph-metadata.json (e.g. hard-coded in advisor scorer projection.ts, command YAML, or the deep-loop-runtime council code) that the graph scan would not catch? This slice only covered graph-metadata + Keywords; a broader grep for the 5 skill-ids in advisor/command code is a separate slice.

readers.slice: convergence + state divergence across deep-ai-council, deep-context, deep-improvement, deep-research, deep-review

findings.observation: The graph-aware convergence engine is ALREADY largely centralized in deep-loop-runtime: a single scripts/convergence.cjs dispatches on loopType ∈ {research, review, context, council}. computeCompositeScore() carries three per-mode weight vectors hardcoded (research: questionCoverage .30/claimVerificationRate .25/...; context: reuseCatalogCoverage .30/agreementRate .25/sliceCoverage .20/...; review: dimensionCoverage .25/findingStability .20/p0ResolutionRate .25/...). evaluateResearch/evaluateReview/evaluateContext each hold their own threshold table + blocker rules. council branches entirely to lib/council/convergence.cjs.
findings.merge_implication: deep-loop-workflows does NOT need to reimplement convergence — it consumes the runtime's loopType-keyed engine. The merge must preserve loopType as the discriminator (research/review/context/council are real, divergent signal sets) and NOT flatten them to one signal vector. Per-mode threshold/weight tables are the per-mode semantics to keep.

findings.observation: deep-ai-council does NOT use the coverage-graph signal path. It routes to lib/council/convergence.cjs with a wholly different signal set (agreementRatio .25, dissentDensity .20, evidenceDepth .20, unresolvedCriticalDisagreements .20, decisionConfidence .15) computed from a separate council-graph-db (DECISION/RECOMMENDATION nodes, AGREES_WITH/CONTRADICTS/EVIDENCE_FOR edges), and an empty graph returns STOP_BLOCKED (vs CONTINUE for the coverage-graph modes). Config convergence_signal is the human label 'two-of-three-agree'.
findings.merge_implication: Council is the single most-divergent lane: different graph DB, different node/edge ontology, different empty-graph semantics, adjudicator-verdict scoring instead of relevance/severity. deep-loop-workflows must treat council as a first-class mode with its own scoring contract, not a variant of the coverage-graph convergence. This is a hard 'preserve, do not flatten' boundary.

findings.observation: deep-improvement is the outlier: it has NO loopType, never calls runtime convergence.cjs, and detects stop entirely via config-driven stopRules — maxConsecutiveTies, stopOnDimensionPlateau + plateauWindow:3, thresholdDelta, trade-off regression thresholds (hard -3/soft -5) — over a 5-dimension weighted score (structural .20/ruleCoherence .25/integration .25/outputQuality .15/systemFitness .15). Convergence is a plateau/tie detector on a candidate-scoring trajectory, plus guarded promotion (promotionEnabled:false, proposalOnly:true, baselineRequired:true).
findings.merge_implication: deep-improvement's convergence is a different KIND (trajectory-plateau scoring, not coverage saturation). It cannot share the runtime's graph convergence engine. deep-loop-workflows must keep a distinct 'improvement' convergence mode whose stop logic is config-driven plateau detection + guarded promotion gating, NOT a coverage-graph loopType. This is the clearest case where a naive flatten would break behavior.

findings.observation: deep-research carries a SECOND convergence layer beyond the runtime graph engine: a newInfoRatio signal model (Rolling Average w0.30 / MAD noise floor w0.35 / Question Entropy w0.35) with the explicit contract 'Signals nominate STOP; legal-stop and graph gates authorize or block it.' progressiveSynthesis:true and machine-owned strategy sections (key-questions, answered-questions, exhausted-approaches, ...) are unique to research.
findings.merge_implication: Research convergence is two-stage (heuristic signal vote → graph gate), not single-stage. The merge must preserve BOTH the newInfoRatio signal layer and the graph gate as research-mode semantics; collapsing to the runtime graph score alone would drop the rolling-average/MAD/entropy stop voting that research relies on.

findings.observation: Each of deep-research, deep-review, deep-context owns its OWN scripts/reduce-state.cjs, and deep-improvement owns scripts/shared/reduce-state.cjs — four separate implementations. deep-review's is review-domain-specific (REQUIRED_DIMENSIONS, SEVERITY_WEIGHTS P0:10/P1:5/P2:1, RESOLVES, resource-map emission, LIVE_LINEAGE_MODES). deep-improvement's is a generic dashboard/registry generator with its OWN atomic-write + jsonl-repair primitives (writeTextAtomic, repairJsonlTailInline) duplicating the runtime's writeStateAtomic/repairJsonlTail.
findings.merge_implication: The reducer layer is genuinely divergent per mode (severity vs relevance vs candidate-score) and must be preserved as per-mode reducers inside deep-loop-workflows, NOT merged into one. BUT the duplicated atomic-write/jsonl-repair primitives in deep-improvement's reducer are a consolidation opportunity — they should call deep-loop-runtime's writeStateAtomic/repairJsonlTail instead of re-inlining. Distinguish domain logic (keep separate) from durability plumbing (centralize to runtime).

findings.observation: Each skill ships a distinct config schema and fileProtection map, encoding per-mode state files. review: deep-review-state.jsonl(append-only)+deltas+deep-review-findings-registry.json+strategy/dashboard, tunable {convergenceThreshold .10/stuckThreshold 2/maxIterations 7}. research: deep-research-state.jsonl+research/research.md(mutable)+.deep-research-pause sentinel+findings-registry, threshold .05/stuck 3/iter 10. context: context/context-report.{md,json}+findings-registry+fanout by-model-shared-scope+relevanceGate .55+agreementMin 2, threshold .10/iter 8. improvement: agent-improvement-state.jsonl+improvement-journal.jsonl(2 separate ledgers)+experiment-registry+candidates/+benchmark-runs/. council: ai-council/** session/topic/round hierarchy (session_id/topic_id/round_id), append_only_state, max_rounds 3, schema_version deep-ai-council.config.v1.
findings.merge_implication: State-file layouts are NOT unifiable into one schema — they are the per-mode contracts. deep-loop-workflows must keep five mode-scoped config schemas + fileProtection maps. Common spine to factor out: {maxIterations, convergenceThreshold, stuckThreshold, executor block, status, lineage, _optimizerManaged tunable/locked registry} appears in 4/5 (council uses round-based rather than iteration-based). Council's session/topic/round hierarchy and improvement's dual-ledger are the two shapes that resist a shared iteration-NNN spine.

findings.area: optimizer-managed tunable/locked registry (shared governance, per-mode fields)
findings.observation: research/review/context configs each carry an identical _optimizerManaged block pointing at one shared optimizer-manifest.json, with tunableFields uniformly [convergenceThreshold, stuckThreshold, maxIterations] but lockedFields that differ per mode (review locks reviewDimensions+reducer+fileProtection; research locks lineage+reducer; promotionMode advisory-only). council and improvement configs have NO _optimizerManaged block.
findings.evidence: deep_review_config.json:85-91; deep_research_config.json:70-76; deep_context_config.json (no block); deep_ai_council_config.json (no block); improvement_config.json (no block)
findings.merge_implication: The offline optimizer integration is a partially-adopted cross-cutting concern (3/5 modes). The merge should either extend it uniformly to all 5 modes or document council/improvement as intentionally opt-out — but it must preserve the per-mode lockedFields divergence (each mode protects different state from the optimizer). A flat shared locked-list would let the optimizer mutate fields a given mode considers immutable.

open_questions: Does deep-improvement intentionally bypass the runtime convergence.cjs (no loopType registered), or is it a pre-runtime-extraction holdover that should gain an 'improvement' loopType? The plateau/trajectory model may not map onto the coverage-graph signal abstraction at all.

deep-research has two convergence layers (newInfoRatio signal vote + graph gate); is the signal-vote layer implemented in deep-research/scripts (not read this sweep) or also in runtime? Confirms whether the signal layer is skill-local code that must migrate into deep-loop-workflows vs already shared.

Council's empty-graph=STOP_BLOCKED vs coverage-graph empty=CONTINUE is a deliberate semantic inversion — confirm no caller assumes a uniform empty-graph decision across modes before the merge collapses dispatch.

The four reduce-state.cjs files share helper scaffolding (readUtf8/parseJson/atomic-write) — quantify true duplication vs domain divergence (severity weights, dimensions, candidate scoring) to scope which parts centralize to runtime vs stay per-mode in deep-loop-workflows.
Only research/review/context wire _optimizerManaged; decide whether the merge extends optimizer governance to council/improvement or formally marks them opt-out.

readers.slice: deep-improvement deep dive (Lane C skill-benchmark, Lane D non-dev-ai-system, sweep-benchmark, evaluator-first scoring, guarded promotion; references/ + scripts/ inventory)

findings.observation: deep-improvement is by far the largest of the five skills: SKILL.md is 564 lines describing FOUR co-equal lanes (A agent-improvement, B model-benchmark, C skill-benchmark, D non-dev-ai-system), backed by 23 reference docs (references/{agent_improvement,model_benchmark,skill_benchmark,non_dev_ai_system,shared}/), ~55 .cjs scripts across 6 script subtrees (agent-improvement/, model-benchmark/, skill-benchmark/, non-dev-ai-system/, shared/, lib/), plus assets/ for 4 lanes, 10 changelog versions (v1.1.0.0 -> v1.10.0.0 + current v1.16.0.0), and ~15 vitest suites.
findings.merge_implication: If the other four skills (ai-council/context/research/review) are 'modes' with one persona each, deep-improvement alone has FOUR internal lanes — merging it as a single 'mode' of deep-loop-workflows under-models it. The crux: deep-improvement is itself a multi-lane skill, so the merged skill is really N+3 modes, not N+1. The spec must decide whether each deep-improvement lane becomes its own top-level mode or whether deep-improvement stays a nested mode-group.
findings.evidence: .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/SKILL.md:1-564; 4 lanes at SKILL.md:34-41; references listed via Glob (23 files); scripts via Glob (~55 .cjs)

findings.observation: All four lanes route through ONE mode-switching entry point: scripts/shared/loop-host.cjs. VALID_MODES = {agent-improvement, model-benchmark, skill-benchmark, non-dev-ai-system-refine} (loop-host.cjs:45). Lanes share three pluggable seams (candidate-source, dispatcher, scorer) and a shared reducer (scripts/shared/reduce-state.cjs), shared journal (scripts/shared/improvement-journal.cjs), and shared promoter (scripts/shared/promote-candidate.cjs). The agent-improvement default path is byte-identical when no --mode flag is set (loop-host.cjs:16,29).
findings.merge_implication: deep-improvement ALREADY demonstrates the exact 'one host, mode-flag, shared backend' pattern the merge wants. loop-host.cjs is a proof-of-concept for how deep-loop-workflows could dispatch modes. The merge can lean on this seam rather than invent it — but it also means deep-improvement's internal mode-switch (loop-host) overlaps conceptually with the loop YAML/runtime dispatch the merged skill will use. Need to reconcile two layers of mode-routing.

findings.observation: Lane D's loop host is NOT in deep-improvement at all — it lives WITH the packaging under test as a Python file: <packaging-root>/benchmark/_loop/loop.py, plus _gates/gates.py (frozen scoring) and _gates/derive.py (derived-copy regen). deep-improvement only ships a thin Node adapter (scripts/non-dev-ai-system/run-non-dev-ai-system.cjs, 'owns no loop logic') that spawns python3. Lane D has its OWN journal at benchmark/_loop/state/loop-journal.jsonl with 18 event types and 10 stop reasons that MAP ONTO (but differ from) the deep-improvement taxonomy (e.g. noTarget->converged, promotedStop->converged, killSwitch->blockedStop). Pilot is the external 'Barter Copywriter' AI system.
findings.merge_implication: This is the single biggest merge risk. Lane D is a cross-language (Python), cross-repo (packaging-owned, external pilot) contract that deep-improvement only adapts to. It cannot become a clean in-skill 'mode' because its core logic deliberately lives outside the skill so it can evolve independently. The spec should treat Lane D as a contract/adapter boundary that the merged skill references, NOT as code to absorb. Strong candidate to remain a distinct sub-mode (or even stay logically separate) with its own stop-reason translation layer.
findings.evidence: .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/references/non_dev_ai_system/loop_contract.md:14-172; scripts/non-dev-ai-system/run-non-dev-ai-system.cjs:7-32 ('owns no loop logic'); pilot at run-non-dev-ai-system.cjs:18-20

findings.area: Lane C (skill-benchmark) — distinct domain + dual trace modes
findings.observation: Lane C is diagnostic-only (does NOT mutate the target skill — SKILL.md:75) and benchmarks whether a SKILL is routed/discovered/efficient/useful, scoring 5 dimensions D1-D5 with a D5 structural hard gate run first. It has its own 14-script subtree (scripts/skill-benchmark/: run-skill-benchmark, score-skill-benchmark, d4-ablation, d5-connectivity, router-replay, contamination-lint, live-executor, browser-executor, advisor-probe, executor-dispatch, playbook-generator, load-playbook-scenarios, build-report, _args). Two trace modes: --trace-mode router (deterministic CI gate) and --trace-mode live (real cli-opencode dispatch + opt-in D4-R claude-graded task-outcome ablation). It reuses another skill's manual_testing_playbook as its corpus.
findings.merge_implication: Lane C's output (a Skill Benchmark Report) and its non-mutating, skill-introspection nature are conceptually closer to deep-review/deep-context than to agent-improvement's mutate-and-promote loop. Its router-replay/contamination-lint/D5-connectivity machinery is specific to introspecting SKILL.md routers — it does not generalize to the other lanes. Merges as a 'mode' but carries a large self-contained tool subtree that won't share much beyond the loop-host/reducer/journal seam. Candidate for a clearly-bounded sub-mode.
findings.evidence: .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/references/skill_benchmark/operator_guide.md:13-70; diagnostic-by-default at SKILL.md:75; 14 skill-benchmark scripts via Glob

findings.observation: Lane A scores candidates with dynamic-mode 5-dim scoring (Structural 0.20, Rule Coherence 0.25, Integration Consistency 0.25, Output Quality 0.15, System Fitness 0.15 — SKILL.md:279-285), profiles generated on-the-fly via generate-profile.cjs (no static profiles shipped). It has rich runtime-truth machinery: stop-reason taxonomy (6 reasons) + sessionOutcome (4 outcomes), 5 legal-stop gate bundles (contract/behavior/integration/evidence/improvement), mutation-coverage graph with sha256 signature dedup and loop_type:'improvement' namespace isolation, dimension-trajectory plateau detection, Pareto trade-off detector, optional parallel candidate waves, advisory weight optimizer. Note SKILL.md:385 states only 'new' lineage mode is shipped; resume/restart/fork are documented-but-unwired.
findings.merge_implication: This is the deepest, most agent-improvement-specific logic and is the natural 'core mode'. Its 5-dim scorer, gate bundles, and mutation-coverage are tightly coupled to mutating bounded agent .md files and do not transfer to Lanes B/C/D. The merge must preserve this as the canonical agent-improvement mode without diluting its many guardrails. Lowest merge risk per-feature but highest behavioral surface to keep byte-identical (loop-host guarantees byte-identical default path — that contract must survive the merge).
findings.evidence: .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/SKILL.md:275-444; lineage-only-new at SKILL.md:383-389; loop_type isolation at SKILL.md:395

findings.observation: Promotion is shared but lane-aware: scripts/shared/promote-candidate.cjs guards a single canonical-target + archive + runtime-mirror sync for ALL lanes, but has ONE lane-branch — Lane B promotes from a benchmark report (--benchmark-report <report.json>, bypassing the agent-scored-file requirement when status is benchmark-complete) while Lane A promotes a scored candidate (SKILL.md:305). Lane D promotion is entirely external (promote-N into an isolated git worktree, never the live tree, at most one promotion per run, ending at promotedStop). Hardening env gates (DEEP_AGENT_ALLOW_CRITERIA_EXEC, DEEP_AGENT_GRADER_CACHE_RAW) default permissive for trusted-author backward-compat.
findings.merge_implication: The promoter is mostly shared (good for merge) but already carries lane-specific branches, and Lane D promotion lives outside the skill entirely. The merged skill needs a promotion contract that spans an in-skill shared promoter (A/B) AND an external worktree promoter (D) AND a no-promotion diagnostic lane (C). Promotion semantics are NOT uniform across modes — the spec cannot assume one promotion gate fits all modes.
findings.evidence: .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/SKILL.md:289-306; Lane D worktree promotion at references/non_dev_ai_system/loop_contract.md:127-139; hardening rationale at SKILL.md:306

findings.observation: scripts/model-benchmark/sweep-benchmark.cjs is the Lane B matrix sweep + scoring (framework-bakeoff or model-vs-model per memory note), with supporting lib/ (sweep-stats.cjs, sweep-reporter.cjs, correctness-gate.cjs, framework-renderer.cjs, code-task-scorer.cjs, reviewer-scorer.cjs, profile-validator.cjs) and a dedicated scorer/ tree (deterministic/ + grader/ + lib/cache + score-model-variant.cjs, the ported 120/003 5-dim scorer). Lane B benchmark outputs are always written to the sk-prompt-small-model hub (.opencode/skills/sk-prompt-small-model/benchmarks/{run_label}/), NOT spec-local (SKILL.md:373-375).
findings.merge_implication: Lane B has a hard external coupling: it writes benchmark artifacts into a DIFFERENT skill's tree (sk-prompt-small-model/benchmarks/). That cross-skill output path is a hidden dependency the merge must preserve — moving deep-improvement into deep-loop-workflows must not break the sk-prompt-small-model hub contract. Lane B also has the deepest scorer/grader subtree (cache, dispute, hallucination-flag, bundle-gate), much of it ported from spec 120/003. Merges as a mode but drags a large model-grading toolchain and an external write target.
findings.evidence: .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/SKILL.md:298-306,364-375; sweep + scorer scripts via Glob (scripts/model-benchmark/sweep-benchmark.cjs, scorer/ tree)

findings.area: Shared vs lane-specific code split (merge surgery map)
findings.observation: loop-host.cjs explicitly separates LANE_A bare-name resolution (score-candidate, generate-profile, rollback-candidate, candidate-lineage, scan-integration, check-mirror-drift, trade-off-detector, benchmark-stability) from lane dirs, resolving bare names to lane paths at spawn time so the plan stays byte-identical (loop-host.cjs:47-60). Truly shared code is scoped under scripts/shared/ (loop-host, reduce-state, improvement-journal, promote-candidate, mutation-coverage, materialize-benchmark-fixtures, model-family, rubric-guard, parse-args, fixture-lint, extract-deliverable) and scripts/lib/ (typed-errors, promotion-gates, profile-resolve, mirror-sync-verify). Each lane owns an isolated subtree.
findings.merge_implication: The skill is already cleanly partitioned into shared/ + per-lane dirs, which is the BEST possible starting point for the merge — shared/ + lib/ map naturally onto deep-loop-runtime (the existing shared backend), while each lane subtree maps onto a mode in deep-loop-workflows. The spec's phased migration can follow these existing seams almost 1:1. Risk is concentrated in the byte-identical default-path guarantee (loop-host) and the external couplings (Lane B hub, Lane D Python contract), not in disentangling shared from lane code.
findings.evidence: .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/scripts/shared/loop-host.cjs:44-60; scripts/shared/ + scripts/lib/ inventory via Glob; per-lane subtrees agent-improvement/ model-benchmark/ skill-benchmark/ non-dev-ai-system/

findings.observation: deep-improvement defines a canonical stop-reason taxonomy (converged, maxIterationsReached, blockedStop, manualStop, error, stuckRecovery) + sessionOutcome enum, frozen in improvement-journal.cjs STOP_REASONS/SESSION_OUTCOMES (SKILL.md:466-471). Lane D maintains its OWN richer set (noTarget, promotedStop, killSwitch, visibleUnmeasurable, promote_skip) that it explicitly MAPS back onto the canonical taxonomy (loop_contract.md:100-114). Labels like plateau/rejected/deferred are NOT accepted by the CLI validator and must reconcile to canonical reasons.
findings.merge_implication: The journal/stop-reason taxonomy is the de-facto shared vocabulary across all four lanes and already has a translation layer for the divergent Lane D. This taxonomy is a strong candidate to live in deep-loop-runtime as the shared convergence/journal contract for ALL deep-loop modes (it overlaps with the convergence concepts in deep-research/review). The merge should hoist the stop-reason + journal contract into the runtime, and each mode (including the external Lane D loop.py) maps onto it.

open_questions: Do the sibling four skills (ai-council/context/research/review) ALSO have multi-lane internal structure, or is deep-improvement the only multi-lane skill? This determines whether the merged skill's 'mode' axis is flat (5 modes) or hierarchical (modes with sub-lanes).

Does deep-loop-runtime already own a stop-reason/journal/convergence contract that deep-improvement's taxonomy could fold into, or would hoisting it create a conflicting second contract? (Not read in this slice — runtime is out of scope here.)

Is the Lane B -> sk-prompt-small-model/benchmarks/ external write path the only cross-skill output coupling, or do Lanes C/D also write into foreign skill trees? (Lane C writes to a caller-supplied --outputs-dir; Lane D writes under the external packaging root — both appear caller/external, not foreign-skill, but worth confirming.)

Lane D's loop.py lives in an EXTERNAL packaging (Barter Copywriter, outside this repo). Does the merge spec need to coordinate a contract-version bump with that external pilot, or is the adapter the only repo-side surface that changes?

How many command files (/deep:start-*-loop) and loop YAML files back each lane, and do they live in deep-improvement or in the shared command/runtime tree? (Commands referenced at SKILL.md:547-550 but not read in this slice.)

findings.observation: The deep-loop-runtime peer skill was created by the FULL_ISOLATE_NO_MCP consolidation, which moved the shared runtime libraries, coverage-graph ownership (schema/query/signals), script entry points (status/query/upsert/convergence), SQLite storage under database/, and runtime tests into one MCP-FREE backend skill, AND removed the four mcp__mk_spec_memory__deep_loop_graph_* MCP tools, replacing them with direct .cjs process invocation. The runtime explicitly 'owns no agents' — it is pure shared infrastructure consumed by the five workflows (deep-review, deep-research, deep-context, deep-improvement, deep-ai-council via council-scoped modules).
findings.merge_implication: This is the load-bearing precedent the merge must honor: the runtime/workflow split already exists and is intentional. deep-loop-workflows MUST remain a pure consumer of deep-loop-runtime's script entry points and own NO runtime libs, coverage-graph, storage, or tests — those stay in the runtime peer. The merge collapses only the five workflow/persona surfaces, not the runtime boundary. The 'MCP-free, direct .cjs invocation' contract is frozen; the merged skill cannot reintroduce an MCP tool surface.

findings.observation: Each deep-loop workflow ships a native agent the loop YAML dispatches BY NAME (agent: <name>), and every such agent is one canonical source plus two runtime mirrors with identical bodies: .opencode/agents/<name>.md (canonical, OpenCode frontmatter mode: subagent + permission: block), .claude/agents/<name>.md (Claude mirror, tools: allow-list frontmatter), .codex/agents/<name>.toml (Codex mirror, developer_instructions + # Converted from: header + sandbox_mode). The five named agents are deep-context, deep-research, deep-review, deep-improvement, ai-council. A native agent present in .opencode/agents/ but missing its .claude/ or .codex/ mirror SILENTLY fails to dispatch in that runtime (CLI seats still run; only native seats drop). The rule: when adding/renaming a deep-loop native agent, create/update all three files and verify three-way parity (ls .opencode/agents .claude/agents .codex/agents).
findings.merge_implication: Merging the five SKILLS does NOT merge the five AGENTS — each agent name is the YAML dispatch key per runtime, so the five native agents (x3 runtime mirrors = 15 files) likely must persist or be renamed in lockstep across all three runtime dirs. If the merge renames or consolidates any agent, every rename must touch all three mirror files or the native seat silently drops in a runtime. The merged skill's docs must preserve the three-way-parity rule. This is a major migration constraint: agent surface is orthogonal to skill surface.

findings.observation: The loop commands and YAML are SHARED across runtimes: the .claude/ and .codex/ commands/prompts/skills dirs are SYMLINKS to .opencode/, so commands reference canonical .opencode/ paths and dispatch agents by name on purpose — they are explicitly NOT forked per runtime. Packet 143/002 confirms this live: '/deep:start-packaging-refine-loop command (.claude/commands is a symlink; one file serves both runtimes)'. Packet 143/002 also mandates that runtime mirror sync go through 'the repo's normal mirror tooling — not hand-copied'.
findings.merge_implication: Because skills dirs are symlinked .claude/.codex -> .opencode, moving/renaming the skill folder (deep-context etc -> deep-loop-workflows) only needs the canonical .opencode/skills/ change to propagate to all runtimes — but ONLY if the symlink targets are updated/preserved. Commands stay shared and reference canonical .opencode/ paths; the merge must update those references in the single shared command files (not per-runtime). The agents are the exception — they are real mirrors, not symlinks, so they need the three-way file edit while skills/commands ride the symlink.

findings.observation: Packet 134/007 established that ALL 7 deep commands open with two un-skippable, ordered HARD-BLOCK gates: Gate 1 = 'PHASE 0: @GENERAL AGENT VERIFICATION' (STATUS: ☐ BLOCKED, asserts orchestrator context before dispatching sub-agents/CLIs, names that command's own skill + restart line), then Gate 2 = the BLOCKED unified setup phase (STATUS: ☐ BLOCKED; :confirm/no-suffix must present prompt and WAIT; :auto must resolve confidently or fail fast naming missing inputs). Each EXECUTION PROTOCOL lists 'Run Phase 0' as step 1. Enforcement is deliberately MARKDOWN-LEVEL (prompt hard-block), NOT a runtime hook — justified because /deep:* slash commands only ever run in the orchestrator. Verified by grep: rg 'PHASE 0: @GENERAL AGENT VERIFICATION' must match all commands; two ☐ BLOCKED markers per command.
findings.merge_implication: The merged deep-loop-workflows skill's command surface must preserve the two-gate opening (Phase 0 @general + BLOCKED setup, ordered, ☐ BLOCKED markers, EXECUTION PROTOCOL 'Run Phase 0' first action) verbatim in every retained command. The canonical Phase 0 block is a copy-template adapted with 3 substitutions (indicator skill, box skill, restart line) — so any command whose backing skill name changes to deep-loop-workflows must update its Phase 0 skill-name + restart line. The grep-based acceptance gates (PHASE 0 match all, two ☐ BLOCKED markers each) become migration regression checks.

findings.observation: Packet 123/006 set a NON-NEGOTIABLE precedent: a behavior-affecting change to the deep loops must prove a single-executor run is BYTE-IDENTICAL to pre-change main (config, state.jsonl modulo timestamps, iteration md, research.md/review-report.md). The fan-out opt-in policy and SKILL/convergence/runtime doc carve-outs (deep-research SKILL.md §4 NEVER #9 + EXPERIMENTAL, deep-review FORBIDDEN INVOCATION PATTERNS, both convergence.md 'Fan-Out Convergence', deep-loop-runtime SKILL.md script table) were all gated behind this parity proof, and default policy stayed single-executor unchanged. Also: changes were proven structural (skip_when guards + if_absent resolver branch) rather than altering the default path.
findings.merge_implication: The merge is intended to be a DOCS/STRUCTURE reorganization, not a behavior change — so it should be held to the same byte-identical single-executor parity gate: a representative run of each mode (research/review/context/improvement/council) must produce byte-identical artifacts pre/post-merge (modulo timestamps). This gives the migration spec a concrete, established acceptance bar. It also signals the merge must NOT change config/state/iteration output formats — only where the prose/persona content lives.

findings.observation: Packet 143/002 documents the standard documentation surfaces a deep-loop skill must carry, established by deep-improvement's own conventions: feature_catalog/NN--<name>/, manual_testing_playbook/NN--<name>/, a changelog entry (versioned, e.g. v1.11.0.0), README/AGENTS command-table rows, and graph-metadata.json intent signals so skill_advisor routes the intent. New lanes/modes get first-class parity (Layer-2 command + advisor routing + standard doc surfaces), verified by an advisor router replay (>=3 phrasing variants route correctly). The agent definition's lane table and the cli-opencode agent_delegation reference must also enumerate every lane.
findings.merge_implication: deep-loop-workflows must consolidate FIVE skills' worth of these doc surfaces (feature_catalog/NN--*, manual_testing_playbook/NN--*, changelog, graph-metadata intent signals, README/AGENTS rows) into one skill's conventions — renumbering and de-duplicating across the five. Critically, graph-metadata.json intent signals from all five must be unioned into the merged skill so skill_advisor still routes every mode (research/review/context/improvement/council); a router replay (>=3 variants per mode) is the established acceptance check. Losing any mode's intent signals would silently break advisor routing for that mode.

findings.area: fan-out / wave deferral and forbidden-pattern carve-outs
findings.observation: Packet 123/006 shows the deep loops carry explicit FORBIDDEN/EXPERIMENTAL prose: deep-research SKILL.md §4 NEVER list (item #9) and deep-review SKILL.md 'FORBIDDEN INVOCATION PATTERNS' were amended to PERMIT command-driven fan-out while keeping ad-hoc shell fan-out and intra-lineage 'wave-within-lineage' FORBIDDEN/deferred. This is per-skill governance prose that differs between the research and review skills (research uses a NEVER-list, review uses a FORBIDDEN INVOCATION PATTERNS section).
findings.merge_implication: Each of the five skills has its OWN governance/forbidden-pattern prose structured differently (NEVER-list vs FORBIDDEN INVOCATION PATTERNS vs lane tables). The merge must reconcile these into one coherent governance section for deep-loop-workflows WITHOUT dropping any forbidden-pattern carve-out (e.g. 'wave-within-lineage stays deferred', 'no ad-hoc shell fan-out'). These are load-bearing safety invariants; a naive concat or a chosen-one-survives merge risks losing a mode-specific prohibition.

open_questions: Do all five named native agents (deep-context, deep-research, deep-review, deep-improvement, ai-council) keep their current names post-merge, or does the merge rename any? If renamed, each rename is a 3-file (x3-runtime) operation per the mirror convention — confirm against the actual agents dirs before writing the migration spec.

Is the skills dir symlink (.claude/.codex/skills -> .opencode/skills) at the dir level or per-skill? This determines whether renaming five skill folders to one deep-loop-workflows folder needs symlink retargeting or rides the existing dir-level symlink. (Confirmed for commands via 143/002; skills-dir granularity not directly read in this slice.)

Does ask-ai-council's known dual-naming (/speckit:deep-council vs /deep:ask-ai-council, 134/007 limitation #2) get reconciled during the merge, or is it carried forward as-is? It is an existing documented inconsistency the merge will inherit.

Which versioned changelog scheme wins for the merged skill — do the five skills' changelog histories collapse, archive, or carry forward? 143/002 shows each lane gets its own changelog version bump (v1.11.0.0).
